steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Defoe makes two paired claims about what historians must do. When a story has "sufficient authority," the historian must give the world "the special testimonial of its proper voucher" (credit the source). When it lacks authority, the historian must "say so." Both duties share the same principle: the historian must be transparent about how credible each source is.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — all historians are forced to admit some records may be false: Defoe says unverified stories must be labeled as such, not that they are false. He does not claim all historians must confess to falsehoods. ✗
  • Choice B — historians should only quote widely credible figures: Defoe never says historians must exclude weak sources — only that they must flag them. This is too restrictive. ✗
  • Choice C — historians will eventually feel remorse for their work: No mention of remorse anywhere in the passage. ✗
  • Choice D — historians should clearly indicate how trustworthy each source is: This captures Defoe's dual requirement — credit strong sources, flag weak ones — in a single principle: transparency about source credibility. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
